skillName: Money problems involving addition and subtraction
misconceptionSummary: Students don't connect money notation to decimal place value — they see £ and p as separate systems.

visuals.ruleText: £1 = 100p, so 1p = £0.01 — pence are hundredths of a pound
visuals.subText: £4.73 means 4 pounds and 73 hundredths of a pound

steps.narration: Money is a decimal system. One pound is one hundred pence, so each penny is one hundredth of a pound. The digits after the decimal point in a price are always hundredths — pence.

Four pounds seventy-three: four is the whole pounds, seven is the tens of pence which are tenths of a pound, and three is the pence which are hundredths of a pound. The decimal point separates whole pounds from fractions of a pound.

lines.text: £6.34 is close to £6.50 ✓ — sense check passes

steps.narration: Always estimate before calculating. Round each price to the nearest fifty pence or pound and add mentally. If the exact answer is far from the estimate, a mistake has been made.

visuals.label: Pence = hundredths of £ — money obeys decimal place value

steps.narration: Six pounds thirty-four. Money and decimals are the same system — the decimal point is the boundary between whole pounds and fractions of a pound.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: treating pence as a separate number system. Pence are hundredths of a pound — £4.73 is four and seventy-three hundredths, exactly like any other decimal.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Money is a decimal system. Pence are hundredths of a pound — the two digits after the decimal point. All decimal addition and subtraction rules apply directly.
